blocks.body: Phrasal Verbs Basics shows that verb + particle combinations can be literal, semi-literal, or fully idiomatic.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"Phrasal verbs are built from a verb and a particle, but the meaning of the whole expression can be literal or completely idiomatic.","rules":[{"label":"Verb + particle","color":"emerald","text":"The particle after the verb is often a small word such as up, out, on, off or after.","example":"sit down / turn off / wake up"},{"label":"Literal or idiomatic","color":"blue","text":"Sometimes the meaning is easy to guess, and sometimes it is not: go out is almost literal, but give up is idiomatic.","example":"She went out. / He gave up smoking."},{"label":"Context matters","color":"amber","text":"The same verb root with different particles can create different meanings.","example":"pick up a bag / pick up a language / pick up speed"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Core idea and form
blocks.body:
A phrasal verb usually follows the pattern verb + particle, and sometimes verb + particle + preposition.

  • Literal: the direction or physical action is still clear - come in, sit down, go out.
  • Semi-literal: the meaning is slightly extended, but the logic is still visible - wake up, calm down, slow down.
  • Idiomatic: the meaning must be learned as a whole unit - give up, run into, carry on.

blocks.heading: 2. Main uses and examples
blocks.body:
TypeExamplesMeaning
daily actionsget up, sit down, put onsimple routine and movement
search and findinglook for, find out, come acrosssearching, discovering, meeting by chance
change and continuationturn into, grow up, carry onbecoming something, maturing, continuing

When you learn a phrasal verb, save one short sentence with it, not only a dictionary translation.

blocks.heading: 3. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: translating the phrasal verb word by word.
    Better: check the whole expression, for example give up = stop doing something.
  • Mistake: thinking that every verb + preposition combination is a phrasal verb. Some are ordinary verb + preposition structures.
  • Mistake: learning a list with no examples. Without context, phrasal verbs are forgotten much faster.
  • Mistake: ignoring the fact that the same phrasal verb may have more than one meaning in different topics.

blocks.heading: 4. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• Look at the whole expression, not only the verb.
  • Learn each phrasal verb together with an example and a typical object.
  • Not all phrasal verbs are literal, but almost all of them are easier to learn through short everyday situations.
